title: Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ

learningObjectives.text: Explain lexical scope using a new example and justify the result.
Trace and explain scope chain using a new example and justify the result.
Explain variable environment using a new example and justify the result.
Explain hoisting using a new example and justify the result.
Explain temporal dead zone using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in scope, scope chain, hoisting, and the tdz recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **How JavaScript Works** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Lexical scope** → **Scope chain** → **Variable environment** → **Hoisting** → **Temporal Dead Zone**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Lexical scope
sections.body: Lexical scope is determined by where code is written. Inner code can access surrounding bindings, while outer code cannot access bindings declared only inside an inner scope.

sections.title: Scope chain
sections.body: Identifier lookup starts in the current lexical environment and continues outward until a matching binding is found or the global boundary is reached.

sections.title: Variable environment
sections.body: A variable environment records bindings for an execution context. Declaration kinds differ in scope, initialization, and observable behavior before their declaration line.

sections.title: Hoisting
sections.body: Hoisting describes behavior created during environment setup. Function declarations are available early, var starts as undefined, and let/const/class bindings exist but remain uninitialized.

sections.title: Temporal Dead Zone
sections.body: The Temporal Dead Zone lasts from entering a scope until a lexical declaration is initialized. Reading the binding during that period throws a ReferenceError.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The inner function resolves names through local and outer lexical environments.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ connects Lexical scope, Scope chain, Variable environment.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Apply Lexical scope deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Lexical scope, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ?

choices.label: Avoid Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Use Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ.

choices.label: Use Scope, Scope Chain, Hoisting, and the TDZ when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
